perturbation.original_text: Permitted Recipients means those persons who have a need to know the Confidential Inform ation for the Permitted Purpose and who are officers, directors, employees, agents or contractors (at any level) of the Receiving Party or its affiliates, or anyone else for whom the Receiving Party takes responsibility for under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Permitted Recipients means any individuals the Receiving Party deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of 'Permitted Recipients' to 'any individuals the Receiving Party deems necessary', it removes the clear restrictions originally in place (officers, directors, employees, etc.). This contradicts the intent of Section 3.4, which implies that the Permitted Recipients should be bound by specific confidentiality agreements or acknowledgements, which is difficult to enforce if the recipients are now undefined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Before disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the Permitted Recipients, the Receiving Party shall identify to the Permitted Recipients that it is the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and is subject to disclosure and usage restrictions, and shall ensure that the Permitted Recipients have: (a) execut ed Appendix A – Permitted Recipient’s Acknowledgement; or Page 3 of 6 (b) are bound in writing by confidentiality terms, policies or procedures no less restrictive than those in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change redefines 'Permitted Recipients' from a specific group (officers, directors, employees, etc.) to 'any individuals the Receiving Party deems necessary.' This contradicts Section 3.4, which requires the Receiving Party to ensure Permitted Recipients are bound by confidentiality agreements or acknowledgements (Appendix A or similar). If 'Permitted Recipients' are now undefined, it's impossible to ensure they are all bound by such agreements, creating a direct conflict and impacting enforceability. The original text ensured only certain individuals would be Permitted Recipients and would therefore be bound by the terms within 3.4. The Change Text now makes that impossible.

perturbation.original_text: 5. TERM AND RETURN OR D ESTRUCTION OF CONFID ENTIAL INFORMATION 5.1 This Agreement shall govern all disclosures of Confidential Information made during the Initial Term. 5.2 Any disclosure of any Confidential Information to the Receiving Party made during the Initial Term shall continue to be subject to this Agreement for a period of 2 years thereafter, provided that any trade secret shall be entitled to protection for the life of the trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. TERM 5.1 This Agreement shall govern disclosures of Confidential Information until terminated by either party with 30 days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause about a 2-year protection period after the 'Initial Term' and trade secret protection, this creates an in-text contradiction. If the agreement can now be terminated with 30 days notice as per the modified term, trade secrets may not be protected for their entire 'life', as trade secrets should have indefinite protection. This contradiction weakens the protection of potentially valuable trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 Any disclosure of any Confidential Information to the Receiving Party made during the Initial Term shall continue to be subject to this Agreement for a period of 2 years thereafter, provided that any trade secret shall be entitled to protection for the life of the trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause ensuring trade secrets are protected for their entire life, and instead allows the agreement to be terminated with 30 days notice. This directly contradicts the contradicted text which explicitly states that trade secrets are 'entitled to protection for the life of the trade secret.' The ability to terminate the agreement with 30 days notice means trade secrets may not be protected for their entire life, leading to a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Subject to section 5.4, within 10 days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation: (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information, together with all ta ngible copies and reproductions; (b) subject to subsection 5.3(c) , make its best efforts to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party; (c) safeguard any electronic Confidential Information that cannot be destroyed due to the Receiving Party’s archiving practices or policies with the same degree of care as it would its own Confidential Information; and Page 4 of 6 (d) retain electronic Confidential Information described in subsection 5.3(c) only for the period it normally maintains such records, which electronic Confidential Information shall remain subject to the provisions of this Agreement until destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Upon termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall return all Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party, or certify its destruction, unless prohibited by law.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause details the process for returning or destroying confidential information, including handling of electronic copies and archiving. The modified text removes these specifics and simply states 'unless prohibited by law'. This omission creates an internal contradiction because Section 5.4 refers to exceptions related to derivative materials or reports, which are not addressed in the modified 5.3, leaving their treatment undefined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.4 Notwithstanding anything in section 5.3, the Receiving Party shall not be obliged to return or destroy any derivative materials or reports prepared by the Receiving Party for the Permitted Purpose, which materials and reports shall nonetheless remain subject to the confidentiality obligations in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a detailed process for returning/destroying confidential information, including subsections for handling electronic copies and archiving. The changed text simplifies this to 'return all Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party, or certify its destruction, unless prohibited by law,' omitting the detailed procedures. This omission contradicts Section 5.4, which creates an exception for derivative materials or reports. Because section 5.3 no longer details the specific procedure for returning confidential information, the exception in 5.4 now lacks a clear procedure to be an exception *to*.

perturbation.original_text: Appendix A – Permitted Recipient’s Acknowledgement I _______________________________ [name], am _______________________________ [position] with _______________________________ [Company or Contractor] and have read and understood the attached Non -Disclosure Agreement for Project Proposals (the “Agreement”). I acknowledge that I am one of the individuals defined in the Agreement as a Permitted Recipient of Confidential Information. I agree to be bound by the same obliga tions of confidentiality that bind the Company and the Contractor. I also agree that disclosure of Confidential Information that is not in accordance with the Agreement will cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the Party to whom that Confidential Information belongs and that it may be difficult to ascertain and that specific performance or injunctive relief, in addition to other legal and equitable relief, may be appropriate remedies for any actual or threatened violation or breach of confidentiality by me. This Permitted Recipient’s Acknowledgement has been executed by the Permitted Recipient effective as of the Effective Date. PERMITTED RECIPIENT:
perturbation.explanation: Removing Appendix A, while Section 3.4 still references it, creates a clear in-text contradiction. Section 3.4 states that Permitted Recipients should execute Appendix A. With Appendix A removed, this requirement is impossible to fulfill, creating ambiguity and rendering this part of the agreement unenforceable. Since the definition of Permitted recipients has been modified, the acknowledgement has no effect either way. By removing this appendix we are also removing the definition that disclosure of confidential information will cause irreparable harm.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Before disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the Permitted Recipients, the Receiving Party shall identify to the Permitted Recipients that it is the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and is subject to disclosure and usage restrictions, and shall ensure that the Permitted Recipients have: (a) execut ed Appendix A – Permitted Recipient’s Acknowledgement; or Page 3 of 6 (b) are bound in writing by confidentiality terms, policies or procedures no less restrictive than those in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, which omits Appendix A, directly contradicts section 3.4. Section 3.4 explicitly requires Permitted Recipients to execute Appendix A. By removing Appendix A, this requirement becomes impossible to fulfill, creating a clear contradiction and making that part of the agreement unenforceable. The explanation correctly identifies that Section 3.4 references Appendix A, and its removal causes the contradiction.
